sections.concept: Deductive reasoning where conclusions are drawn from two or more statements.
sections.solvingTechnique: Use Venn Diagrams. 'All A are B' (A inside B). 'Some A are B' (Intersection). 'No A is B' (Disjoint).

sections.concept: Positive statements (All, Some) can never lead to Negative conclusions individually. 'Either-Or' condition applies when Subject and Predicate are same and one is +ve, one -ve.
sections.solvingTechnique: Always draw the 'Minimum Overlap' diagram first. Check for 'Possibility' by trying to draw alternate valid diagrams.

examples.question:
🧩 Basics: Statements: All Cats are Dogs. All Dogs are Rats.
Conclusions: I. All Cats are Rats. II. Some Rats are Cats.
examples.solution:
Step 1: Draw Venn: C inside D inside R.
Step 2: C is fully inside R? Yes. (I follows).
Step 3: Can we go from R to C? Some R are C. Yes. (II follows).
Step 4: Both follow.

examples.question:
🧩 Basics: Statements: Some Pens are Books. Some Books are Papers.
Concl: I. Some Pens are Papers. II. Some Papers are Books.
examples.solution:
Step 1: P - inter - B - inter - Pa.
Step 2: P and Pa have no direct link. (I does not follow).
Step 3: Pa and B allow intersection. (II follows).
Step 4: Only II follows.
examples.explanation: No direct link = No definite conclusion.

examples.question:
🧩 Basics: Statements: All Cars are Buses. No Bus is Train.
Concl: I. No Car is Train. II. Some Buses are Cars.
examples.solution:
Step 1: C inside B. B disjoint from T.
Step 2: Since C is inside B, and B touches no T, C can touch no T. (I follows).
Step 3: B contains C. (II follows).
Step 4: Both follow.

examples.question:
🧩 Basics: St: All A are B. All B are C.
Concl: I. Some C are A. II. All C are A.
examples.solution:
Step 1: A inside B inside C.
Step 2: Outer to Inner is 'Some'. Inner to Outer is 'All'.
Step 3: I (Some) follows. II (All) does not follow (C is bigger).
Step 4: Only I follows.

examples.question:
🧩 Basics: St: Some Tables are Chairs. All Chairs are Wood.
Concl: I. Some Wood are Tables. II. No Wood is Chair.
examples.solution:
Step 1: T intersect C. C inside W.
Step 2: Since C is inside W, the part of T matching C is also W. (I follows).
Step 3: Positive statements cannot give Negative conclusion. (II Fail).
Step 4: Only I follows.

examples.question:
🧩 Basics: St: No A is B. No B is C.
Concl: I. No A is C.
examples.solution:
Step 1: A separate B. B separate C.
Step 2: A and C relation? Unknown. Could overlap.
Step 3: Definite conclusion 'No A is C' False.
Step 4: Neither follows.

examples.question:
🧩 Basics: St: All Men are Mortal. Ram is a Man.
Concl: Ram is Mortal.
examples.solution:
Step 1: Ram inside Man inside Mortal.
Step 2: Follows.

examples.question:
🧩 Basics: St: Some Fruits are Apples. No Apple is Orange.
Concl: I. Some Fruits are not Oranges.
examples.solution:
Step 1: F inter A. A no O.
Step 2: The part of F that is A cannot be O.
Step 3: True. Some Fruits (the Apple ones) are not Oranges.

examples.question:
🧩 Basics: St: All P are Q. Some Q are R.
Concl: Some P are R.
examples.solution:
Step 1: P in Q. Q inter R.
Step 2: P and R link unclear.
Step 3: Does not follow.

examples.question:
🧩 Basics: St: All X are Y. All Z are Y.
Concl: Some X are Z.
examples.solution:
Step 1: X in Y. Z in Y.
Step 2: X and Z could be separate inside Y.
Step 3: Does not follow.

examples.question:
⚙️ Exam Level: St: Some A are B. Some B are C.
Concl: I. Some A are C. II. No A is C.
examples.solution:
Step 1: A-B-C. A and C no direct relation.
Step 2: Indiv I fails. Indiv II fails.
Step 3: But A and C must either overlap or not.
Step 4: Subject/Predicate same. One +ve, One -ve.
Step 5: Either I or II follows.

examples.question:
⚙️ Exam Level: St: All A are B. Some B are not C.
Concl: Some A are not C.
examples.solution:
Step 1: A in B. Part of B is not C.
Step 2: Does that part include A? Maybe, Maybe not.
Step 3: A could be fully inside C (compatible with B not fully inside C).
Step 4: Does not follow.

examples.question:
⚙️ Exam Level: St: No P is Q. All R are P.
Concl: No R is Q.
examples.solution:
Step 1: R inside P. P separate from Q.
Step 2: R cannot touch Q.
Step 3: Follows.

examples.question:
⚙️ Exam Level: St: Some Dogs are Rats. All Rats are Cats. No Dog is Pig.
Concl: I. Some Cats are Dogs. II. No Pig is Rat.
examples.solution:
Step 1: D inter R. R in C. D no P.
Step 2: R is part of C. R inter D. So C inter D. (I Follows).
Step 3: P no D. R is touching D. Can P touch R? Yes, non-D part of R. Or maybe not. Is 'No Pig is Rat' definite? No.
Step 4: Only I follows.

examples.question:
⚙️ Exam Level: St: All Windows are Doors. All Entrances are Windows.
Concl: I. All Entrances are Doors. II. Some Doors are Entrances.
examples.solution:
Step 1: E in W. W in D. -> E in D.
Step 2: I Follows (Direct).
Step 3: II Follows (Outer to Inner Some).
Step 4: Both follow.

examples.question:
⚙️ Exam Level: St: Some A are B. All B are C. No C is D.
Concl: I. No B is D. II. Some A are not D.
examples.solution:
Step 1: A-B. B in C. C no D.
Step 2: B is inside C. C no D. So B no D. (I Follows).
Step 3: Part of A is B. That part is C. That part cannot be D. So Some A are not D. (II Follows).
Step 4: Both follow.

examples.question:
⚙️ Exam Level: St: All P are Q. Some Q are R.
Concl: I. All P being R is a possibility.
examples.solution:
Step 1: Standard diagram: P in Q. Q inter R (outside P).
Step 2: Possibility diagram: P inside Q. R intersects Q such that it swallows P.
Step 3: Valid? Yes, statements hold. 
Step 4: Follows.

examples.question:
⚙️ Exam Level: St: No A is B. No B is C.
Concl: I. All A being C is a possibility.
examples.solution:
Step 1: A sep B. B sep C.
Step 2: Can A be inside C? Yes, as long as neither touches B.
Step 3: Follows.

examples.question:
⚙️ Exam Level: St: All A are B. No A is C.
Concl: I. Some B are not C.
examples.solution:
Step 1: A in B. A no C.
Step 2: The part of B that is A cannot be C.
Step 3: So Some B (the A part) are not C. True.
Step 4: Follows.

examples.question:
⚙️ Exam Level: St: Some X are Y. Some Y are Z.
Concl: I. No X is Z. II. Some X are Z.
examples.solution: Step 1: Either I or II follows.

examples.question:
🚀 Placement Level: Only a few A are B. Some B are C.
Concl: I. All A can be B. II. Some A are not B.
examples.solution:
Step 1: 'Only a few A are B' = Some A are B AND Some A are NOT B.
Step 2: I. All A can be B? No, because 'Some A are NOT B' is a condition. False.
Step 3: II. Some A are not B. True (Definition).
Step 4: Only II follows.

examples.question:
🚀 Placement Level: St: Only A are B. Some A are C.
Concl: I. All B can be C. II. Some C are B.
examples.solution:
Step 1: 'Only A are B' = All B are A AND B cannot belong to anyone else (B is exclusive to A).
Step 2: I. Can B be C? No, B is exclusive to A. (False).
Step 3: II. Some C are B? No. (False).
Step 4: Neither follows.
examples.explanation: 'Only X are Y' = All Y are X (Exclusive).

examples.question:
🚀 Placement Level: St: 0% A are B. 100% B are C.
Concl: I. Some C are not A.
examples.solution:
Step 1: 0% = No. 100% = All.
Step 2: No A is B. All B are C.
Step 3: B is inside C. B cannot be A.
Step 4: So the part of C that is B cannot be A.
Step 5: Follows.

examples.question:
🚀 Placement Level: St: Some A are B. All C are D. No B is C.
Concl: I. Some A are not C. II. All D can never be B.
examples.solution:
Step 1: A-B. B sep C. C in D.
Step 2: I. Part of A is B. B cannot be C. So that part of A cannot be C. (Follows).
Step 3: II. All D be B? If All D is B, then C (which is in D) becomes B. But No B is C. Contradiction. So D cannot be fully B. (Follows).
Step 4: Both follow.
examples.explanation: Can never be = Not a possibility.

examples.question:
🚀 Placement Level: St: All A are B. Common B are C? No. All A are B. No B is C.
Concl: I. No A is C. II. Some B are A.
examples.solution:
Step 1: A in B. B sep C.
Step 2: I. A is inside B. B no C. So A no C. (True).
Step 3: II. B contains A. Some B are A. (True).
Step 4: Both follow.

examples.question:
🚀 Placement Level: Reverse Syllogism: Concl: Some Chairs are Tables.
Which statements imply this?
A) No Chair is Table.
B) Some Chair is Wood. All Wood is Table.
C) All Table is Wood. No Wood is Chair.
examples.solution:
Step 1: Check B. Chair-Wood. Wood in Table. -> Chair overlaps Table.
Step 2: B implies conclusion.

examples.question:
🚀 Placement Level: St: Some A are B. No B is C. All D are A.
Concl: I. Some D are not C. II. All D are B is a possibility.
examples.solution:
Step 1: D in A. A inter B. B sep C.
Step 2: I. D is in A. Does D touch B? Not necessarily. Does D touch C? Can D be C? C cannot check B. D is A. A can touch C. So D can be C. 'Some D are not C' is not definite. (Fail).
Step 3: II. Can All D be B? B and D are inside/part of A. Yes, D can go inside the A-B intersection. (Follows).
Step 4: Only II follows.

examples.question:
🚀 Placement Level: St: Only a few Pink are Red. Only a few Red are Blue.
Concl: I. Some Pink are Blue. II. No Pink is Blue.
examples.solution:
Step 1: P inter R (with restriction). R inter B (restriction).
Step 2: P and B relation unknown.
Step 3: I Fail. II Fail.
Step 4: Either I or II follows (Complementary pair).

examples.question:
🚀 Placement Level: St: All A are B. All C are B.
Concl: I. Some A are C is a possibility. II. No A is C is a possibility.
examples.solution:
Step 1: A and C both inside B. They might overlap or not.
Step 2: Possibility of overlap? Yes (I True).
Step 3: Possibility of no overlap? Yes (II True).
Step 4: Both follow.

examples.question:
🚀 Placement Level: St: At least some A are B. Mostly B are C.
Concl: Some A are C.
examples.solution:
Step 1: At least some = Some. Mostly = Some.
Step 2: A-B-C.
Step 3: A-C linkage? No.
Step 4: Does not follow.

shortcuts: Subject & Predicate same + One Pos/One Neg + Both Fail = Either-Or.
'Only X is Y' -> All Y is X (and Y cannot touch others).
'Can never be' is NOT a possibility question, it is a Definite Negative question.
commonMistakes: Mistake: Thinking 'Some A are not B' follows from 'Some A are B'. (It doesn't).
Mistake: Diagramming 'Only A is B' as 'All A is B'. (It is All B is A).
Mistake: Missing the 'Either-Or' pair and marking 'Neither'.
placementTips: In possibility, try to force the condition True. If no statement violated, it's possible.
Learn keywords: 'Ideally', 'Seldom', 'A few' = Some. 'None but' = Only.
